title: Cascading OOM Kills
summary: API pods are being OOM-killed and restarting in a rolling wave — as each one dies, its traffic shifts to the survivors and kills them faster. The cluster is eating itself. A VP of engineering wants you to break the cascade before there's nothing left serving traffic.

persona.name: Dana Whitfield
persona.description: A VP of engineering who wants the cascade stopped first and the elegant fix second. Asks whether the problem is load or a leak. Impatient with anyone chasing the perfect root cause while pods keep dying; calmer once someone proposes a way to halt the death spiral even if it means temporarily shedding load.

stakes: Each lost pod accelerates the next failure; if the cascade isn't broken the entire API tier goes dark, turning a degradation into a total outage during peak traffic.
hidden_facts: A deploy three hours ago introduced a per-request memory leak that only bites under sustained load, so pods slowly bloat until they OOM.
The cascade is a thundering-herd effect: each death concentrates load and speeds up the survivors' failure.
Rolling back the leaky deploy stops the leak, but the cascade must also be broken — temporarily shedding or rate-limiting traffic lets pods stabilize.
Simply raising the memory limit delays the OOM by minutes but does not stop it; it is not a fix.
objectives: Distinguish a memory leak from genuine overload using the slow-bloat pattern
Break the cascade by shedding or rate-limiting load so survivors stabilize
Roll back the leaky deploy as the real fix rather than just raising limits
Give the VP a clear two-step plan: stop the cascade, then stop the leak
opening_line: Pods are dying faster than they come back and we're running out of cluster. Is this load or a leak — and how do we stop the spiral?
